explanation_md: When fewer arguments than parameters are provided, the compiler fills in defaults from right to left. `show(1)` uses `b=10` and `c=20`; `show(1,2)` uses `c=20`; `show(1,2,3)` overrides everything.
C is invalid because `y` has no default but follows `x` which does. Once a parameter has a default, all parameters to its right must also have defaults. A, B, and D are all valid.

options.a: The call `print3(3, 7)` passes 3 as `a` and 7 as `b`, giving output `3 7 0` — you cannot skip `b` to reach `c`.
options.b: Default arguments cannot be used with functions that have three parameters.
options.c: The value 7 is implicitly converted to a float, causing incorrect output.
options.d: The default value for `b` overrides the passed value, always printing 10.

explanation_md: Arguments are matched left to right. `print3(3, 7)` sets `a=3` and `b=7`, leaving `c` at its default `0`. There is no way to skip `b`; you must call `print3(3, 10, 7)` to get the intended output.

prompt_md: Where should the default argument be placed when a function is declared in a header and defined in a `.cpp` file?

options.a: On the definition in the `.cpp` file only.
options.b: On the declaration in the header file only.
options.c: On both the declaration and the definition.
options.d: It does not matter — the compiler finds it in either place.

explanation_md: Callers only see the header (declaration), so the default must appear there. Placing it only on the definition means callers won't know about it and will get a compile error when they omit the argument. Putting it on both is also a compile error (redeclaration of a default).
